text: The surgeon accesses the abdominal cavity using a Hasson port, with two additional 5mm ports placed in the right upper quadrant and a 12mm port in the left upper quadrant.
An ethensor retractor is positioned to retract the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field.
During the exploration of the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes significant inflammation and edema around the second and third portions of the duodenum.
The surgeon performs careful dissection using a high-energy device, visible through the laparoscopic view, focusing on the area surrounding the second and third portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon performs the Cocker maneuver to mobilize the duodenum, allowing for better access and visualization of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon traces the bile duct, revealing a mass of tissue adjacent to the duodenum, extending from the bile duct toward the third portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon performs dissection of the soft tissues surrounding the duodenum, using a high-energy device to carefully separate inflamed and edematous tissue.
The surgeon identifies a duodenal diverticulum adhered to the second and third portions of the duodenum, with the apex bent toward the bile duct, showing the side walls plastered together.
The surgeon carefully dissects and peels the duodenum diverticulum away from the duodenum, separating the adherent tissue layers using precise techniques.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection techniques to circumferentially separate the diverticulum from the surrounding duodenal tissue, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon peels the diverticulum off the duodenum, revealing a perforation near the base of the diverticulum.

The surgeon dissects the most cephalic aspect of the diverticulum adjacent to the gallbladder, revealing the perforation with a significant amount of contents. The base of the diverticulum is being dissected using a high-energy device.
The surgeon focuses on the most caudal aspect of the diverticulum, which is visually distinct from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon mobilizes the diverticulum and duodenum, ensuring the head of the pancreas remains visible. The diverticulum is observed to originate from the lateral aspect of the duodenum along the side wall, rather than medially at the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs further dissection on the lateral aspect of the diverticulum, aiming to identify its base. The most cephalic aspect of the diverticulum is dissected until the neck is visually identified.
The surgeon uses a 60mm mechanical stapler to perform a diverticulectomy at the clearly identified neck of the diverticulum, applying three staple fires to excise the structure.
The surgeon removes the dissected diverticulum specimen from the abdominal cavity, completing the excision process.
The surgeon inspects the surgical area, examining the site where the diverticulum was previously mobilized and removed.
The surgeon performs a leak test using saline and air on the staple line of the diverticulum, confirming no visible leakage.
The surgeon places a drain in the subhepatic area, ensuring proper postoperative fluid management.
